stage.name: Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving
stage.description: A system-boundary lesson for interrupt and debug interactions around CVA6.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: External events need precise internal landing zones

content.title: External events need precise internal landing zones
content.subtitle: Interrupt and debug are integration topics, but they still end in disciplined architectural state and control flow.
content.thesis: Name the external block, the CVA6 input, and the internal trap or control path separately.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Interfaces
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual lists AXI, Debug, Interrupt, and TRI interface applicability across CVA6 configurations.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson is Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving. Interrupt and debug are integration topics, but they still end in disciplined architectural state and control flow. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

actions.text: This lesson is Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving. Interrupt and debug are integration topics, but they still end in disciplined architectural state and control flow. The point is not to memorize a wall of CVA6 facts. The point is to build a disciplined source-reading habit: name the boundary, name the source, and avoid claims that the public material does not support.

scenes.title: Interrupts are external events with internal consequences

content.title: Do not confuse interrupt source with trap handling
content.subtitle: The interface is external; the architectural response is internal.

columns.title: External context
columns.body: CLINT and PLIC-style blocks can provide timer, software, or external interrupt signals.

columns.title: CVA6 context
columns.body: CVA6 records trap state and redirects control flow according to privilege and CSR state.

content.takeaway: An interrupt explanation has two sides.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Interfaces
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual lists AXI, Debug, Interrupt, and TRI interface applicability across CVA6 configurations.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: interface requirements
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document names MEM, DBG, IRQ, XIF, and TRI requirements, including AXI memory interface, CLINT interrupt interface, CV-X-IF, and P-Mesh TRI.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and identifies trap-related CSR state and return paths.

content.speakerNotes: Interrupts are easy to explain badly. The system has sources such as CLINT and PLIC-style infrastructure. CVA6 has architectural trap state and control-flow response. Those are connected, but not identical. A professional lesson names both sides: where the event comes from, and how the core records and handles it.

actions.text: Interrupts are easy to explain badly. The system has sources such as CLINT and PLIC-style infrastructure. CVA6 has architectural trap state and control-flow response. Those are connected, but not identical. A professional lesson names both sides: where the event comes from, and how the core records and handles it.

scenes.title: Debug scope is not the whole debug system

content.title: Keep the debug module boundary honest

items.title: External module?
items.body: The requirements note allows an external debug module outside IP scope.

items.title: Testharness path?
items.body: The APU testharness shows debug AXI request and response wiring.

items.title: Core consequence?
items.body: Debug control eventually interacts with architectural control and state.

content.takeaway: Do not teach external debug infrastructure as if it were all inside CVA6.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Interfaces
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual lists AXI, Debug, Interrupt, and TRI interface applicability across CVA6 configurations.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 requirements: interface requirements
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The requirements document names MEM, DBG, IRQ, XIF, and TRI requirements, including AXI memory interface, CLINT interrupt interface, CV-X-IF, and P-Mesh TRI.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 APU: testharness
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The testharness connects CVA6, debug, CLINT, PLIC, timer, ROM, UART, DRAM, and other slaves through AXI-related structures.

content.speakerNotes: Debug is another boundary lesson. The user manual says interface applicability depends on configuration, and the requirements note says there can be an external debug module outside the IP scope. The APU testharness gives a concrete wiring example. That is enough to build a correct mental model without pretending this short course replaces the debug specification.

actions.text: Debug is another boundary lesson. The user manual says interface applicability depends on configuration, and the requirements note says there can be an external debug module outside the IP scope. The APU testharness gives a concrete wiring example. That is enough to build a correct mental model without pretending this short course replaces the debug specification.

scenes.title: Use CLINT and PLIC diagrams as system context

content.title: The testharness shows interrupt infrastructure around the core
content.body: CLINT and PLIC are not ordinary execute units; they are mapped system components whose signals and accesses influence core-visible interrupt behavior.
content.caption: CLINT and PLIC diagrams help learners separate external infrastructure from core pipeline logic.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 APU: testharness
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The testharness connects CVA6, debug, CLINT, PLIC, timer, ROM, UART, DRAM, and other slaves through AXI-related structures.

content.speakerNotes: CLINT and PLIC diagrams are useful because they stop us from treating interrupts as a mysterious pin. They are system components with their own mapping and access paths. CVA6 sees the effect through interrupt inputs and trap logic. The practical learner takeaway is to ask: is this a system-block issue, a CSR/trap-state issue, or a pipeline redirection issue?

actions.text: CLINT and PLIC diagrams are useful because they stop us from treating interrupts as a mysterious pin. They are system components with their own mapping and access paths. CVA6 sees the effect through interrupt inputs and trap logic. The practical learner takeaway is to ask: is this a system-block issue, a CSR/trap-state issue, or a pipeline redirection issue?

scenes.title: Check understanding: Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving

questions.question: What is the safest way to teach interrupts in CVA6?

options.label: Separate external source, core input, CSR trap state, and control redirection
Call every interrupt a branch mispredict

questions.analysis: A precise interrupt explanation crosses a boundary and must name each side.

questions.question: Which items belong to interrupt/debug boundary thinking?

options.label: Trap CSRs such as EPC/cause/vector state

questions.analysis: BHT replacement policy is frontend prediction, not interrupt/debug integration.

questions.question: Why should the external debug module be mentioned carefully?

options.label: It may be outside the CVA6 IP scope
It is the same as the scoreboard

questions.analysis: The requirements note explicitly allows an external debug module outside IP scope.

questions.question: What is a good first question when debugging an interrupt issue?

options.label: Is the problem in system source, core input, CSR state, or pipeline response?
Is the logo the right size?
Can the branch predictor store data?

questions.analysis: Boundary classification makes interrupt debug tractable.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks the core judgment of Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving. The right answers should feel less like trivia and more like disciplined classification: what is the boundary, what is the source, what is the limitation, and what would we check next?

scenes.title: Summary: Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving

content.title: Interrupt and debug boundary: external events without hand-waving
takeaways: Explain what belongs to CVA6, what belongs to external interrupt/debug infrastructure, and what must remain source-scoped.
Turn every public explanation into an evidence-backed learning artifact.
content.nextStep: Next: CV-X-IF, where custom instruction extension becomes an execute-stage and scoreboard contract.
content.closing: This is how OpenHW Explorer can teach serious open hardware without drifting into unsupported certainty.

content.speakerNotes: To summarize: Explain what belongs to CVA6, what belongs to external interrupt/debug infrastructure, and what must remain source-scoped. Keep the claim small, keep the source visible, and keep the boundary honest. Next: CV-X-IF, where custom instruction extension becomes an execute-stage and scoreboard contract.
